does: This paper introduces Stream, a hierarchical pruning algorithm that enables mechanistic interpretability analysis of attention patterns in long-context language models by leveraging sparse attention. The core contribution is Sparse Tracing, which computes per-head sparse attention masks in near-linear time O(T log T) and linear space O(T) through binary-search-style refinement of top-k key blocks per query. Stream preserves the model's next-token behavior while pruning 97-99% of token interactions, making interpretability computationally feasible for million-token contexts where traditional quadratic-scaling techniques become infeasible, and the method is validated on long chain-of-thought reasoning traces and the RULER benchmark for analyzing information retrieval paths.
connects: This work extends mechanistic interpretability methods, which have traditionally focused on analyzing attention and other model components in smaller contexts, to the regime of modern million-token LLMs where standard analysis techniques hit severe memory and computational walls. It builds on prior sparse attention mechanisms in transformer models and connects to recent work on scaling mechanistic interpretability beyond toy problems. The paper responds to the practical challenge that detailed attention analysis becomes prohibitive as context length grows, combining insights from sparse transformer architectures with the precision requirements of interpretability research to create a technique that maintains interpretability properties while operating under real computational constraints.
enables: Stream opens the door to systematically understanding how long-context reasoning actually works in state-of-the-art LLMs, enabling researchers to trace information flow across million-token contexts and identify which token interactions are mechanistically essential. The method could accelerate work on layer-wise analysis of reasoning, studying how models route information from distant contexts to output predictions, and identifying failure modes in long-context behavior. By making attention analysis practical at scale, Stream could support debugging and improving long-context capabilities, understanding hallucination and retrieval failures in extended reasoning tasks, and validating whether models actually implement faithful reasoning chains. The one-pass compilable nature suggests potential for real-time interpretability tools during model inference.
